step.title: Step 182
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 9 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 431, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at (32,32), 4px. 'G' frame at (28,32) keeps regenerating from split 'b' parts - infinite loop. Need to stop and approach 'c' frames instead.
- Plan: Move LEFT 12, UP 4 to approach 'c' frame at (28,16) from the right side.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 430 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 6.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The player moved LEFT 12 times, but only 9 actions were executed. The player is now at (32, 0), which means the player moved all the way to the left edge of the board.
Looking at the ascii:
Row 28: wwwwccccwwwwwwwwbbbbccccbbbbwwwwwwwwwww
Row 29: wwwwcbbcwwwwwwwwbgggcbbcgggbwwwwwwwwwww
Row 30: wwwwcbbcwwwwwwwwbgggcbbcgggbwwwwwwwwwww
Row 31: wwwwccccwwwwwwwwbbbbccccbbbbwwwwwwwwwww
Row 32: wwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwww
The 'c' frame at (28, 16) is still there, and a new 'c' frame appeared at (28, 32) - this is the 'G' frame that transformed back to 'c' when the player moved away!
Wait, looking more carefully:
- c: pos=(28,16), pixels=12 (original left 'c' frame)
- c: pos=(28,32), pixels=12 (the 'G' frame transformed back to 'c' when player moved away)
- b: pos=(28,28), pixels=10 (left split 'b' part)
- b: pos=(28,36), pixels=10 (right split 'b' part)
So the 'G' frame at (28, 32) transformed back to 'c' when the player moved away. This is interesting - the 'G' frame only appears when the player is close.
The player is at (32, 0), which is at the left edge of the board. The 'N' object is at (32, 1), which is next to the player.
I need to rethink my approach. The player moved too far left. Let me move RIGHT to get back to a reasonable position, then approach the 'c' frame at (28, 16) properly.
The player is at (32, 0). The 'c' frame is at (28, 16). I need to move RIGHT 16 times to reach col 16, then UP 4 times to reach row 28.
But wait, the player is at col 0, and the 'c' frame is at col 16. Moving RIGHT 16 times would put the player at col 16, which is the same column as the 'c' frame. I should approach the frame from the right side, so I should move RIGHT to col 20 (4 columns right of the frame), then UP to row 28.
Actually, let me reconsider. The 'c' frame is at (28, 16), occupying rows 28-31, cols 16-19. To approach it from the right, I should move to col 20, then UP to row 28.
The player is at (32, 0). Moving RIGHT 20 times would put the player at col 20. Then moving UP 4 times would put the player at row 28.
But wait, the batch was supposed to be LEFT 12, UP 4, but only 9 LEFT actions were executed (the player moved from col 32 to col 0, which is 32 columns, not 12). This is strange.
Oh wait, looking at the output: "Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT" - only 9 LEFT actions were executed, but the player moved from col 32 to col 0. That's 32 columns, not 9.
Hmm, maybe the player is moving faster when there's no obstacle? Or maybe the player wrapped around the board?
Actually, looking at the ascii, the player is at (32, 0), and the 'N' object is at (32, 1). The player is at the left edge of the board.
Wait, maybe the player moved LEFT 9 times from col 32, which would put the player at col 23 (32 - 9 = 23). But the output says the player is at (32, 0). This doesn't add up.
Let me re-examine. The player was at (32, 32) before the action. The action was LEFT 12, UP 4. But only 9 LEFT actions were executed. If the player moved LEFT 9 times from col 32, the player would be at col 23.
But the output says the player is at (32, 0). This is very strange.
Oh wait, maybe the player moved LEFT and wrapped around the board? Or maybe the player moved LEFT and the board scrolled?
Actually, looking at the ascii output, the player is at row 32, and the 'N' object is at row 32, col 1. The player is at col 0. So the player is at the left edge of the board.
I think the issue is that the batch was interrupted. The action was LEFT 12, UP 4, but only 9 LEFT actions were executed before the batch stopped. The player moved from col 32 to col 0, which is 32 columns. This doesn't make sense for 9 LEFT actions.
Wait, maybe the player is moving 4 columns per LEFT action when there's no obstacle? That would explain it: 9 * 4 = 36, but the player moved from col 32 to col 0, which is 32 columns. Hmm, that doesn't quite add up either.
Actually, I think the issue is that the batch was stopped early because one of the actions had no impact (the player hit a wall or something). Let me check the result.
Looking at the output: "Result: gameplay_changed=True, level_completed=False, done=False". The gameplay changed, so the actions were not all no-impact.
Let me just focus on the current state and plan from here. The player is at (32, 0). I need to move RIGHT to approach the 'c' frame at (28, 16).
Let me move RIGHT 20 times to reach col 20, then UP 4 times to reach row 28. This should put the player adjacent to the right side of the 'c' frame.
But I'm concerned about the player moving too far. Let me first check the current state more carefully.
Actually, looking at the ascii again:
Row 32: wwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwwww
The player is not visible in this row! The player is at (32, 0), but the ascii shows 'w' at row 32, col 0.
Wait, the ascii output is for cols 12-50, not cols 0-50. So the player at col 0 is not visible in this crop.
Let me check the full ascii for row 32.
Actually, the segmentation says the player (W) is at pos=(32,0), pixels=4. So the player is at row 32, col 0, occupying a 2x2 square.
OK, so the player is at the left edge of the board. I need to move RIGHT to approach the 'c' frame at (28, 16).
Let me move RIGHT 20 times to reach col 20, then UP 4 times to reach row 28.
But I'm worried about the player moving too fast again. Let me try a smaller batch first: RIGHT 10, UP 4.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player at (32,0), 4px - moved too far left. 'c' frame at (28,16) still there. 'G' frame at (28,32) transformed back to 'c' when player moved away.
Plan: Move RIGHT to approach 'c' frame at (28,16). Try RIGHT 20, UP 4.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Move RIGHT 20, UP 4 to approach 'c' frame at (28,16)
result = action(['RIGHT']*20 + ['UP']*4)
print(f"Result: gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}, done={last_action_result.get('done')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("\nKey objects:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] <= 50:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min = min(r for r,c in b)
        c_min = min(c for r,c in b)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pos=({r_min},{c_min}), pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check ascii around player
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nAscii rows 24-40, cols 12-50:")
for r in range(24, 41):
    print(f"Row {r}: {lines[r][12:51]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
